constitutional_posts.text: Which Articles deal with the Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) of India?
constitutional_posts.explanation: The CAG is mentioned in Articles 148 to 151.

constitutional_posts.text: Who is the current Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG) of India?
constitutional_posts.explanation: Girish Chandra Murmu is the current CAG of India.

constitutional_posts.text: Who acts as the highest law officer of the Government of India?
constitutional_posts.explanation: The Attorney General (Mahanayavadi) is the highest law officer of the Government of India.

constitutional_posts.text: Who is the current Attorney General of India?
constitutional_posts.explanation: R. Venkataramani is serving as the current Attorney General of India.

constitutional_posts.text: What is the role of the Solicitor General of India?
constitutional_posts.explanation: The Solicitor General assists and is subordinate to the Attorney General.

constitutional_posts.text: Who is the current Solicitor General of India?
constitutional_posts.explanation: Tushar Mehta is currently the Solicitor General of India.

constitutional_posts.text: The position equivalent to Attorney General at the State level is?
constitutional_posts.explanation: The Advocate General is the highest law officer at the State level, akin to the AG at the Center.

constitutional_posts.text: Who is the current Advocate General of Rajasthan?
constitutional_posts.explanation: Rajendra Prasad (Gupta) is the current Advocate General of Rajasthan.

constitutional_posts.text: Which post is covered by Articles 148 to 151?
constitutional_posts.explanation: Articles 148-151 cover the Comptroller and Auditor General (CAG).

constitutional_posts.text: R. Venkataramani holds which of the following offices?
constitutional_posts.explanation: R. Venkataramani is the Attorney General of India.

constitutional_posts.text: Girish Chandra Murmu serves as?
constitutional_posts.explanation: Girish Chandra Murmu is the CAG of India.

constitutional_posts.text: The chief function of the Solicitor General is to assist the:
constitutional_posts.explanation: The Solicitor General assists the Attorney General in their duties.

constitutional_posts.text: Which body audits the accounts of the Union and the States?
constitutional_posts.explanation: The CAG audits accounts at both the Union and State levels.

constitutional_posts.text: What is the Hindi term for Attorney General?
constitutional_posts.explanation: Attorney General is called 'Mahanayavadi' in Hindi.

constitutional_posts.text: What is the Hindi term for Advocate General?
constitutional_posts.explanation: Advocate General is translated as 'Mahadhivakta'.

constitutional_posts.text: What is the Hindi term for Comptroller and Auditor General?
constitutional_posts.explanation: CAG is termed 'Niyantrak evam Mahalekha Parikshak'.

constitutional_posts.text: Who represents the Central Government in complex legal cases in the Supreme Court primarily?
constitutional_posts.explanation: The Attorney General primarily represents the Govt of India in the Supreme Court.

constitutional_posts.text: Which article (though not explicitly listed in notes, fundamentally related) appoints the AG?
constitutional_posts.explanation: Article 76, part of the Executive chunk (52-78), establishes the Attorney General.

constitutional_posts.text: An Advocate General is appointed for a?
constitutional_posts.explanation: An Advocate General is appointed individually for every State.

constitutional_posts.text: Rajasthan's Advocate General Rajendra Prasad (Gupta) operates primarily at?
constitutional_posts.explanation: As Advocate General for Rajasthan, he represents the state in the Rajasthan High Court.

constitutional_posts.text: The term 'Mahanayavadi' belongs to which constitutional post?
constitutional_posts.explanation: Mahanayavadi means Attorney General.

constitutional_posts.text: Who amongst the following is currently holding the second highest law officer post in India?
constitutional_posts.explanation: Tushar Mehta is the Solicitor General, considered the second highest law officer.

constitutional_posts.text: Which constitutional post evaluates public expenditure?
constitutional_posts.explanation: The CAG evaluates and audits public expenditure.

constitutional_posts.text: Which officer's jurisdiction extends specifically across all of India regarding governmental legal advice?
constitutional_posts.explanation: The Attorney General advises the Union government across India.

constitutional_posts.text: CAG stands for?
constitutional_posts.explanation: CAG stands for Comptroller and Auditor General.

lok_sabha.text: Which Article of the Constitution establishes the Parliament of India?
lok_sabha.explanation: Article 79 outlines the constitution of Parliament.

lok_sabha.text: The Parliament of India consists of which of the following?
options: President, Lok Sabha, and Rajya Sabha
Lok Sabha, Rajya Sabha, and Supreme Court
lok_sabha.explanation: The Parliament comprises the President, the Lok Sabha, and the Rajya Sabha.

lok_sabha.text: Which committee recommended that the President be considered a part of the Parliament?
lok_sabha.explanation: The N. Gopalaswami Ayyangar Committee recommended making the President an integral part of Parliament.

lok_sabha.text: Why is the President considered a part of the Parliament?
options: No bill becomes a law without his assent
He is the head of the majority party
lok_sabha.explanation: Because no bill passed by the chambers can become law without the President's approval.

lok_sabha.text: Which Article specifically details the structure of the Lok Sabha?
lok_sabha.explanation: Article 81 details the composition of the Lok Sabha.

lok_sabha.text: Which of these is NOT an alternative name for the Lok Sabha?
lok_sabha.explanation: The Upper House refers to the Rajya Sabha, not the Lok Sabha.

lok_sabha.text: Why is the Lok Sabha called the 'Temporary House'?
options: It functions for only a few days a year
It does not have real power
It is housed in a temporary building
lok_sabha.explanation: It is called a Temporary House because it can be dissolved by the President before its term ends.

lok_sabha.text: When was the Parliament of India first constituted after independence?
lok_sabha.explanation: The first Parliament was constituted in April 1952 following the first general elections.

lok_sabha.text: What was the initial English name of the Lok Sabha?
lok_sabha.explanation: It was initially named the 'House of People'.

lok_sabha.text: When was the name 'House of People' changed to 'Lok Sabha'?
lok_sabha.explanation: The name was changed to Lok Sabha on 14 May 1954.

lok_sabha.text: During the first general elections (1951-52), what was the maximum strength of the Lok Sabha?
lok_sabha.explanation: The maximum strength of the Lok Sabha during the first general elections was set at 500.

lok_sabha.text: How many seats were actually contested in the first Lok Sabha elections?
lok_sabha.explanation: Elections were held for 489 seats.

lok_sabha.text: During the first general elections, how many triple-member constituencies existed?
lok_sabha.explanation: There was exactly one triple-member constituency (in North Bengal).

lok_sabha.text: When was the multi-member constituency system abolished?
lok_sabha.explanation: The multi-member system was abolished in 1961, before the 1962 elections.

lok_sabha.text: The 7th Constitutional Amendment (1956) increased the Lok Sabha seats from 500 to?
lok_sabha.explanation: The 7th Amendment raised the max seats from 500 to 525 after state reorganization.

lok_sabha.text: The 31st Constitutional Amendment (1973) increased the seats to?
lok_sabha.explanation: The 31st Amendment increased the seats from 525 to 545.

lok_sabha.text: Which act set the maximum limit of Lok Sabha seats to 552?
options: Goa, Daman and Diu Reorganisation Act (1987)
lok_sabha.explanation: The Goa, Daman and Diu Reorganisation Act of 1987 set the limit at 552.

lok_sabha.text: According to the 84th Constitutional Amendment (2002), Lok Sabha seats are frozen until which year?
lok_sabha.explanation: The seats are frozen until the year 2026.

lok_sabha.text: Before the 104th Amendment, how many Anglo-Indians could the President nominate to the Lok Sabha?
lok_sabha.explanation: Under Article 331, the President could nominate 2 Anglo-Indians.

lok_sabha.text: Which constitutional amendment discontinued the nomination of Anglo-Indians to the Lok Sabha?
lok_sabha.explanation: The 104th Constitutional Amendment (December 2019) ended the Anglo-Indian nomination.

lok_sabha.text: What is the maximum current possible strength of the Lok Sabha after the 104th Amendment?
lok_sabha.explanation: The maximum strength is now 550 (552 minus the 2 Anglo-Indian seats).

lok_sabha.text: What is the current base census year used to decide population thresholds for Lok Sabha seat distributions?
lok_sabha.explanation: The distribution of seats is frozen based on the 1971 census until 2026.

lok_sabha.text: Who is known as the 'Popular House'?
lok_sabha.explanation: The Lok Sabha relies on direct popular election, hence it is the Popular House.

lok_sabha.text: In the 1951-52 elections, how many double-member constituencies were there?
lok_sabha.explanation: There were 86 double-member constituencies.

lok_sabha.text: How many total constituencies (electoral districts) were there in the first general elections?
lok_sabha.explanation: There were 401 constituencies (314 single, 86 double, 1 triple) yielding 489 seats.

parliament.text: What is the nomenclature for the Legislature in India?
parliament.explanation: In India, the Legislature is known as Sansad or Parliament.

parliament.text: What is the name of the Parliament in the United States of America?
parliament.explanation: In America, the Parliament is called Congress.

parliament.text: What is the name of the Parliament in Japan?
parliament.explanation: Japan's parliament is known as the Diet.

parliament.text: What is the name of the Parliament in Russia?
parliament.explanation: The Russian Parliament is known as the Duma.

parliament.text: Which country's parliament is called 'National People's Congress'?
parliament.explanation: China's parliament is the National People's Congress.

parliament.text: What is the Parliament of Pakistan called?
parliament.explanation: Pakistan's parliament is known as the National Assembly.

parliament.text: 'Jatiya Sangsad' is the parliament of which neighboring country?
parliament.explanation: Bangladesh's parliament is called Jatiya Sangsad.

parliament.text: 'Rastriya Panchayat' is associated with the parliament of?
parliament.explanation: Nepal's parliament is traditionally known as Rastriya Panchayat.

parliament.text: What is the parliament of Afghanistan known as?
parliament.explanation: Afghanistan's parliament is known as Shura.

parliament.text: 'Majlis' is the name of the parliament in which two countries?
parliament.explanation: Iran and Maldives name their parliaments Majlis.

parliament.text: What is the Parliament of Germany called?
parliament.explanation: The German parliament is the Bundestag.

parliament.text: 'Knesset' is the parliament of which country?
parliament.explanation: Israel's parliament is called the Knesset.

parliament.text: Who designed the Old Parliament House of India?
parliament.explanation: The Old Parliament House was designed by Edwin Lutyens and Herbert Baker.

parliament.text: When was the foundation stone of the Old Parliament laid?
parliament.explanation: The foundation stone of the Old Parliament was laid on 12 February 1921 by the Duke of Connaught.

parliament.text: Who inaugurated the Old Parliament House in February 1927?
parliament.explanation: Lord Irwin inaugurated the Old Parliament House in February 1927.

parliament.text: What was the shape of the Old Parliament House?
parliament.explanation: The Old Parliament House had a circular, 'O' shaped layout.

parliament.text: What is the alias given to the Indian Parliament building?
parliament.explanation: The Parliament is also famously known as the 'Heart of the Nation'.

parliament.text: When did the terrorist attack on the Indian Parliament take place?
parliament.explanation: The Parliament was attacked on 13 December 2001.

parliament.text: Who is the principal architect of the New Parliament House?
parliament.explanation: Bimal Patel is the architect of the New Parliament House under the Central Vista Project.

parliament.text: What is the shape of the New Parliament House?
parliament.explanation: The New Parliament House has a triangular shape.

parliament.text: How many members can the Lok Sabha hall of the New Parliament accommodate?
parliament.explanation: The Lok Sabha hall in the new building can seat 888 members.

parliament.text: What is the theme of the Lok Sabha hall in the New Parliament?
parliament.explanation: The Lok Sabha hall features a Peacock theme (National Bird).

parliament.text: What is the theme of the Rajya Sabha hall in the New Parliament?
parliament.explanation: The Rajya Sabha hall features a Lotus theme (National Flower).

parliament.text: Where are joint sessions of the Parliament held in the New Parliament?
parliament.explanation: Joint sessions are now held in the Lok Sabha Hall, which can expand to fit 1272 members.

parliament.text: What is the 'Sengol' installed near the Lok Sabha Speaker's chair?
options: A symbol of righteousness and justice
parliament.explanation: The Sengol is a symbol of righteousness or new justice, derived from the Tamil word 'Semmai'.

union_executive.text: Part V of the Indian Constitution is related to?
union_executive.explanation: Part V of the Constitution deals with the Union Government.

union_executive.text: Articles 52 to 151 of the Constitution belong to which Part?
union_executive.explanation: Part V of the Constitution spans from Article 52 to 151.

union_executive.text: Which part of the Indian Constitution is the largest?
union_executive.explanation: Part V is the largest part of the Indian Constitution.

union_executive.text: What is the primary function of the Legislature (Parliament)?
union_executive.explanation: The main function of the Legislature is to formulate and make laws.

union_executive.text: What is the primary function of the Executive?
union_executive.explanation: The Executive organ of the government is responsible for implementing laws.

union_executive.text: Which organ of the government is responsible for reviewing and interpreting laws?
union_executive.explanation: The Judiciary interprets and reviews laws made by the Legislature.

union_executive.text: The Executive segment inside Part V spans from which articles?
union_executive.explanation: The Executive in Part V covers Articles 52 to 78.

union_executive.text: Which of the following is NOT a part of the Union Executive?
union_executive.explanation: The Chief Justice of India is the head of the Judiciary, not the Union Executive.

union_executive.text: The Legislature (Parliament) in Part V is covered under which articles?
union_executive.explanation: The Parliament (Legislature) is described in Articles 79 to 123.

union_executive.text: The Union Judiciary (Supreme Court) is detailed from Articles?
union_executive.explanation: The Supreme Court falls under Articles 124 to 147 in Part V.

union_executive.text: How many total Parts are there originally in the Indian Constitution?
union_executive.explanation: The Indian Constitution originally consisted of 22 Parts.

union_executive.text: How many total Articles were there initially in the Constitution?
union_executive.explanation: The Constitution originally had 395 Articles.

union_executive.text: Which part of the Constitution is considered the smallest?
union_executive.explanation: Part XX is the smallest part of the Constitution.

union_executive.text: Part XX of the Indian Constitution contains how many Articles?
union_executive.explanation: Part XX contains only one article, which is Article 368.

union_executive.text: Article 368 resides in which part of the Constitution?
union_executive.explanation: Article 368, dealing with amendments, resides in Part XX.

union_executive.text: Who famously referred to the Indian Constitution as 'Paradise of Lawyers'?
union_executive.explanation: Ivor Jennings called the Indian Constitution a 'Paradise of Lawyers' due to its legal complexity.

union_executive.text: What does the term 'Paradise of Lawyers' imply?
union_executive.explanation: It implies that the Constitution's language is highly legalistic and complex, creating a need (and paradise) for lawyers.

union_executive.text: Which part is the longest part in the Constitution?
union_executive.explanation: Part V, dealing with the Union, is the longest part of the Constitution.

union_executive.text: Which article begins Part V of the constitution?
union_executive.explanation: Part V begins at Article 52 with the President of India.

union_executive.text: Part V concludes with the provisions of which office?
union_executive.explanation: Part V concludes with the CAG provisions (Articles 148-151).

union_executive.text: Which constitutional concept interprets laws passed by the Parliament?
union_executive.explanation: The Judiciary reviews and interprets the laws.

union_executive.text: The Union Executive includes all except?
union_executive.explanation: The CAG is covered in a separate chapter of Part V (Arts 148-151) and isn't part of the core executive consisting of Pres, VP, PM, Council, AG.

union_executive.text: Article 79 to 123 pertains primarily to?
union_executive.explanation: Articles 79-123 belong to Chapter II of Part V, dealing with Parliament.

union_executive.text: Article 124 uniquely begins the chapter on?
union_executive.explanation: Article 124 establishes the Supreme Court of India.

union_executive.text: The quote 'Paradise of Lawyers' was made by?
union_executive.explanation: Sir Ivor Jennings coined this phrase analyzing the Indian Constitution.
